source.title: Non-Condensable Gas System Explosion at PCA DeRidder Paper Mill

actions_taken: OSHA issued citations to PCA for emergency response violations
PCA posted flammable atmosphere warning labels on all vessels
PCA revised hot work policies to require draining/purging of vessels within 35 feet before hot work
PCA conducted formal hazard analysis on foul condensate tank and associated processes
PCA evaluated flammable atmosphere prevention mechanisms for replacement tank
DeRidder mill operated post-incident without foul condensate tank via piping and control modifications
event.summary: On February 8, 2017, at approximately 11:05 am CDT, a foul condensate tank at the PCA DeRidder containerboard mill in DeRidder, Louisiana exploded during the annual maintenance outage, killing three contractor workers and injuring seven others. The tank, part of the non-condensable gas (NCG) system, contained water, a layer of flammable turpentine, and an explosive vapor space. Air entered the tank through the pressure vacuum breaker during normal level cycling and during outage cooling, mixing with turpentine vapor to form an explosive atmosphere. Hot work (welding) being conducted on water piping above the tank provided the probable ignition source. PCA had not conducted a process hazard analysis on the foul condensate tank, had not applied its PSM system to the NCG system, lacked instrumentation to detect explosive conditions inside the tank, and had not effectively communicated the explosion hazard to workers or contractors.
recommendations: Apply effective process safety management systems to NCG system equipment using CCPS guidelines
Expand PSM program boundaries beyond minimum legal requirements
Apply NFPA 69 Standard on Explosion Prevention Systems
Apply NFPA 68 Standard on Explosion Protection by Deflagration Venting where explosions cannot be prevented
Ensure safety instrumented systems achieve desired risk reduction per ISA-84
Apply TAPPI TIP 0416-09 for effective NCG system design and operation
Provide workers with periodic training on process safety hazards and hot work safety conditions
OSHA should amend PSM Standard to cover atmospheric storage tanks interconnected to covered processes with 10,000 pounds of flammable substance (reiterated recommendation 2001-05-I-DE-R1)

hazards.name: Flammable/explosive non-condensable gases and turpentine vapor in foul condensate tank
Air ingress into CNCG system creating explosive atmosphere

threats.name: Air ingress through pressure vacuum breaker during normal level cycling
Air ingress during outage cooling of foul condensate tank
Turpentine accumulation due to closed valve on weir recovery system
Hot work (welding) conducted above tank containing explosive atmosphere

consequences.name: Tank explosion causing fatalities and injuries to workers
Projectile hazard from tank launched approximately 375 feet
Structural damage to pipe rack, piping, powerhouse stack work platform, and surrounding process equipment
Release of toxic NCG compounds to atmosphere

controls.name: Pressure Vacuum Breaker (tank overpressure/vacuum protection)
controls.barrier_role: Protect tank from physical damage during pressure or vacuum excursions
controls.lod_basis: Designed to relieve pressure or vacuum conditions exceeding safe operational limits; however, vacuum relief function introduced air into tank

supporting_text: Air most likely entered through the tank's pressure vacuum breaker (Figure 15), as designed, to relieve the low-pressure condition it was experiencing during swings in the foul condensate tank level.
During the 12 hours of operation captured in Figure 26, process data indicate that the pressure vacuum breaker opened enough for air to enter the foul condensate tank at least 14 times.
As the tank level decreased from 20 to 16 feet, the internal pressure decreased, pulling as much as 1,800 cubic feet of air through the vacuum breaker.

controls.name: CNCG System Vacuum Ejectors (maintaining NCG above UEL)
controls.barrier_role: Maintain slight vacuum on CNCG system to collect NCG and keep concentration above upper explosive limit
controls.lod_basis: Ejectors use steam to create slight vacuum drawing off NCG and preventing air accumulation

supporting_text: Mill personnel shut off the ejectors on the CNCG system on February 7, 2017 in the afternoon. Therefore, the CNCG system was not pulling a vacuum from the CNCG header.
During the outage, however, the vacuum system was off, which could allow NCG to accumulate in the vapor space of the tank.

controls.name: Internal Weir for Turpentine Removal
controls.barrier_role: Skim and remove accumulated turpentine from foul condensate tank to reduce fuel load
controls.lod_basis: Automatic level cycling from 16 to 20 feet designed to skim turpentine into weir and send to turpentine recovery system

supporting_text: A valve remained closed in the piping designed to direct any skimmed turpentine to the mill's turpentine recovery system. As a result, the mill did not use the downstream turpentine recovery system for approximately three months.
Because this valve remained closed, turpentine collection in the foul condensate tank was not controlled, and PCA removed the turpentine only when the tank was periodically emptied.
Turpentine likely was accumulating inside the foul condensate tank for approximately three months leading up to the February 8, 2017 incident.

controls.name: Hot Work Permit Program
controls.barrier_role: Identify and control ignition sources and explosive atmospheres before commencing hot work
controls.lod_basis: PCA policy requiring hot work permit, atmospheric monitoring, and elimination of flammables within 35 feet before welding

supporting_text: PCA's hot work permit program was not designed or intended to identify and control the explosive atmosphere inside the foul condensate tank, despite its close proximity to the planned hot work.

The permit focused on the water piping, pipe bridge, and surrounding platform, however, the permit did not consider the potential hazards within adjacent vessels or piping, such as inside the foul condensate tank.
The PCA employees involved in issuing the hot work permit were familiar with the operation of the process, but they did not know that the vapor inside the tank was explosive.

controls.name: Combustible Gas Monitoring (pre-work atmospheric testing)
controls.barrier_role: Detect flammable atmosphere in work area before and during hot work
controls.lod_basis: PCA practice requiring combustible gas detector check of work area before issuing hot work permit

supporting_text: Another PCA employee used a gas detector to check for the presence of flammables around and inside the water piping. With no indications of a flammable atmosphere in the water piping or the platform around the hot work area, PCA issued the hot work permit.
No one checked the inside of the foul condensate tank for a flammable atmosphere.

Continuous combustible gas monitoring provided a weak safeguard against the February 8 incident, for primarily two reasons. First, continuous monitoring assumes that a leak of combustible gas to the atmosphere would occur in the vicinity of the monitoring location; however, the tank still could have exploded without such a leak.

controls.name: Fire Watch during Hot Work
controls.barrier_role: Monitor for flammable atmosphere and fire during hot work operations
controls.lod_basis: PCA and OSHA requirements for continuous combustible gas monitoring and fire watch during welding

supporting_text: While work was progressing, the contractor's fire watch conducted gas monitoring for a flammable atmosphere at ground level. Leading up to the explosion, there is no indication that he detected a flammable atmosphere.
From his position on the ground, the fire watch could not monitor for flammables above the tank, on or near the catwalk where contractors were standing while conducting hot work.

No fire watch was assigned to the elevated hot work area, and the three workers who were killed were located on top of the tank and did not perform any independent gas monitoring while finishing the second task related to hot work.

controls.name: Physical Separation / Isolation of Hot Work from Foul Condensate Tank
controls.barrier_role: Separate ignition sources from explosive atmosphere using barriers, distance, or isolation
controls.lod_basis: NFPA 51B 35-foot rule and PCA hot work procedure requiring removal or shielding of combustibles within 35 feet

supporting_text: The foul condensate tank sat within 35 feet of the hot work, and the exterior of the tank served as the primary barrier between the flammable atmosphere and the hot work. This situation was inappropriate.
During welding, a fire blanket appears to have been used. It was placed over part of the top of the tank. However, this fire blanket did not protect the entire tank from an ignition source.

PCA failed to eliminate the flammable atmosphere inside the tank and also failed to adequately separate the hot work from the flammable atmosphere by using a barrier, such as a fire blanket between the tank and the hot work.

controls.name: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) for NCG System
controls.barrier_role: Identify, evaluate, and control process hazards including explosive atmosphere scenarios in foul condensate tank
controls.lod_basis: Industry good practice and OSHA PSM standard requiring PHA for processes with highly hazardous chemicals

supporting_text: The DeRidder mill never conducted a process hazard analysis to identify, evaluate, and control process hazards for the non-condensable gas system.

PCA did not conduct a hazard analysis of the foul condensate tank, did not effectively assign or communicate clear ownership of the tank to any operations group, and did not install instrumentation on the tank that was capable of detecting when the vapor inside the tank entered an explosive range.
Explosion hazards associated with the foul condensate tank could have been readily revealed by applying a process hazard analysis, one of the basic elements of a process safety management program.

controls.name: Valve Isolation of Water Piping from Foul Condensate Tank
controls.barrier_role: Isolate water piping repair area from foul condensate tank to prevent ignition path
controls.lod_basis: PCA operations personnel closed and locked valves to isolate water piping; air gap created between flanges

supporting_text: PCA did not require - and the contract workers did not install - a positive isolation device, such as a blind flange, on the open end of the valve after disconnecting it from the water piping.
Blinding, a more robust isolation approach, was not used for the foul condensate side of the water piping.

Workers consistently informed the CSB that before starting welding operations, PCA closed and locked the valve on the foul condensate tank side of the water piping. The valve was never located after the explosion.

controls.name: Rupture Disks on CNCG Piping
controls.barrier_role: Prevent explosion overpressure damage to CNCG piping system
controls.lod_basis: Rupture disks located in CNCG piping to relieve overpressure from explosion

supporting_text: Rupture disks are also located in the CNCG piping systems to prevent explosion overpressure damage.
Examination of the header and the rupture disks shows no breakage or other damage.

controls.name: Flame Arrestors on CNCG System
controls.barrier_role: Prevent flame propagation through CNCG piping to other connected units
controls.lod_basis: Flame arrestors near gas source and discharge designed to halt flame front propagation

supporting_text: CNCG system piping is typically equipped with flame arrestors near the gas source, such as the foul condensate tank, and at the gas discharge, such as the boilers used at the PCA DeRidder mill to destroy the NCG.

Flame arrestors in the CNCG header system are designed to prevent fire in this piping from spreading to other connected units and, if unchecked, possibly causing a catastrophic fire and explosion as a flame front propagates through the piping and ignites additional fuel sources.

controls.name: Emergency Response Plan
controls.barrier_role: Coordinate emergency response to minimize harm following an explosion
controls.lod_basis: DeRidder mill emergency response plan for managing post-incident activities

supporting_text: The emergency response plan in place at the DeRidder mill was not properly implemented across the entire facility. According to the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA), a few employees were not evacuated from the facility for more than an hour, no head count was taken of the contractors, and some of the telephones in the mill were not working.
On August 3, 2017, OSHA issued citations to PCA for those violations.

controls.name: Overflow Seal Leg (water seal on overflow piping)
controls.barrier_role: Prevent air ingress into foul condensate tank through overflow piping
controls.lod_basis: Liquid seal leg on overflow piping designed to prevent air ingress while allowing overflow protection

supporting_text: The foul condensate tank was equipped with overflow piping to protect it from a high liquid level. The design of the overflow piping provided for a liquid seal leg to prevent the ingress of air into the tank.
Pressure data from the tank leading up to the incident nonetheless indicated that the overflow piping on the foul condensate tank had a water seal.
The PCA DeRidder mill did not equip this water seal with a makeup water line and did not provide instrumentation for operators to monitor its liquid level.

controls.name: Process Safety Management System (PSM) applied to NCG system
controls.barrier_role: Systematic management of process hazards including NCG explosion risks through PSM elements
controls.lod_basis: OSHA PSM standard and CCPS guidelines for managing highly hazardous chemical processes

supporting_text: PCA did not voluntarily apply its process safety management system to the non-condensable gas system.
The DeRidder mill management did not apply its process safety management system to the CNCG system, which contributed to the explosion.

Although not required by Federal regulation, good-practice guidance recommends developing and implementing a robust safety management system to manage the hazards related to generating, collecting, and treating of non-condensable gases.

controls.name: Fixed Instrumentation for Vapor Space Monitoring inside Tank
controls.barrier_role: Continuously monitor oxygen concentration and explosive atmosphere inside foul condensate tank vapor space
controls.lod_basis: Fixed instrumentation sampling from tank headspace to detect explosive conditions

supporting_text: PCA had not installed instrumentation to analyze the composition of the foul condensate tank vapor space, contributing to PCA not understanding that the vapor space could be explosive.
The most effective way to detect the explosive atmosphere would have been to conduct continuous monitoring inside the foul condensate tank using fixed instrumentation.
PCA did not install instrumentation on the tank that was capable of detecting when the vapor inside the tank entered an explosive range.

controls.name: Tank Draining, Purging, and Inerting during Outage
controls.barrier_role: Eliminate explosive atmosphere in foul condensate tank before maintenance outage and hot work
controls.lod_basis: Industry good practice requiring draining, purging, and inerting of tanks before hot work or maintenance

supporting_text: For the 2017 annual outage, PCA did not empty the liquid in the foul condensate tank.
PCA employees thought that the remaining 20 feet of vapor space contained primarily NCG and water vapor with insufficient air to support combustion.

A properly conducted PHA would have alerted personnel to expect the tank to contain flammable gas. With this knowledge, PCA should have purged and inerted the foul condensate tank during the annual outage.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.

pif_references.PIF-001: Situational awareness / competence gap: safety coordinator and contractors unaware that foul condensate tank vapor space was explosive; fire watch positioned at ground level unable to monitor elevated work area
pif_references.PIF-002: Procedures: hot work permit procedure not designed to identify explosive atmospheres in adjacent vessels; 35-foot rule not effectively applied to foul condensate tank
pif_references.PIF-003: Safety culture / training: PCA did not broadly communicate NCG explosion hazards; operators incorrectly believed air could not enter the sealed system; no training on foul condensate tank hazards provided to contractors
pif_references.PIF-004: Supervision / organisational: unclear ownership of foul condensate tank between pulp mill and powerhouse operations groups; PSM boundaries drawn to exclude NCG system; resource limitations during outage cited as reason for not de-inventorying tank
